| EX | export-skill | 6 | ✓ | — | ✓ |
| RS | rename-skill | 3 | — | — | ✓ |
| DS | drop-skill | 3 | — | — | ✓ |

**Frontmatter Compliance (all 14 workflows):**

- [x] `name:` — ✓ matches folder name in all 14
- [x] `description:` — ✓ present in all 14
- [x] `web_bundle:` — ✓ present in all 14 (all set for web bundling)
- [x] `installed_path:` — ✓ present in all 14, consistent pattern `{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/{workflow}`

**Step File Naming:**

- Primary steps use `step-NN-name.md` pattern — ✓
- Sub-steps use `step-NNx-name.md` letter-suffix pattern (b, c, d) — acceptable convention for optional/tier-gated sub-steps. Examples:
  - `analyze-source/step-01b-continue.md`
  - `create-skill/step-02b-ccc-discover.md`, `step-03b-fetch-temporal.md`, `step-03c-fetch-docs.md`, `step-03d-component-extraction.md`
  - `setup-forge/step-01b-ccc-index.md`
  - `test-skill/step-04b-external-validators.md`
- Pattern appears deliberate for conditional steps gated on capability tier (e.g., CCC-only steps in Forge+/Deep tiers).

**Step Frontmatter (sampled):**

- [x] `name:` present — ✓ (e.g., `step-01-load-brief`, `step-02b-ccc-discover`)
- [x] `description:` present — ✓
- [x] `nextStepFile:` present — ✓ (enables JIT step chaining)
- [x] Additional context files (e.g., `forgeTierFile`, `preferencesFile`) declared as frontmatter variables — ✓ pattern

**Step File Size:**

Largest files (lines): 425 (extraction-patterns.md data), 399 (export-skill/step-04-update-context.md), 391 (rename-skill/step-02-execute.md), 326 (skill-sections.md data), 301 (create-skill/step-03-extract.md). Data files are reference documents and appropriately larger. Step files peak at ~400 lines — within acceptable range for BMAD step files but approaching the size where splitting into sub-steps would aid JiT loading economy.

**Agent Integration:**

- [x] All 14 workflows bound to the `forger` agent (Ferris) via menu triggers in `forger.agent.yaml` — ✓
- [x] Trigger codes match the `[XX]` prefixes in `workflows/README.md` — ✓ (SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, VS, RA, US, AS, TS, EX, RS, DS)
- [x] Cross-workflow coordination documented in `Typical Flows` section of README

**Workflow Mode Distribution:**

- Architect mode: 8 workflows (SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, RA, plus part of SS)
- Surgeon mode: 1 (US)
- Audit mode: 3 (VS, AS, TS)
- Delivery mode: 1 (EX)
- Management mode: 2 (RS, DS)

Balanced coverage across the four-mode operating model declared in the agent persona — ✅ coherence between agent and workflow layer.

**Issues Found:** None.

**Advisory Notes:**

1. **ℹ️ Large step files approaching limit** — `export-skill/step-04-update-context.md` (399), `rename-skill/step-02-execute.md` (391), `drop-skill/step-01-select.md` (298). Consider splitting if they grow further — JiT economy breaks down when a single step file exceeds ~400 lines because the whole file must be loaded before action.
2. **ℹ️ Sub-step letter convention** — `step-NNx-*.md` is not explicitly documented in BMB workflow conventions (as far as `data/` shows). Consider either documenting the pattern in `workflows/README.md` (mention briefly that letter suffixes denote tier-gated sub-steps) or proposing upstream. Current usage is internally consistent, which is the important thing.
3. **ℹ️ `rename-skill` and `drop-skill` have no `data/` folder** — this is appropriate (both are lightweight management workflows with self-contained logic), but it differs from the pattern of the other 12. No action needed — just a data-point for consistency hunters.

**The following built workflows can be validated in detail via sub-process:**

All 14 workflows are eligible for `bmad:bmb:workflows:workflow-validate` deep validation. They have previously been validated individually (see existing reports in `_bmad-output/bmb-creations/`: validation-report-{workflow}-2026-03-26.md for 10 of them).

---

## Documentation Validation

**Status:** PASS (with one structural note)

**Root Documentation (repo-level):**

- **README.md** (133 lines) — ✓ present at repo root
- **CHANGELOG.md** (52 lines) — ✓ present
- **CONTRIBUTING.md** (268 lines) — ✓ present
- **CONTRIBUTORS.md** — ✓ present
- **LICENSE** (MIT) — ✓ present
- **TODO.md** — ⚠️ not found (BMB expects one for modules in active development; SKF tracks roadmap items via git/issues/claude-mem instead — acceptable for mature standalone module)

**README.md Quality Check:**

- [x] Module name and description — ✓ "Skill Forge (SKF) — Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow"
- [x] Problem statement — ✓ "The Problem" section
- [x] Installation instructions — ✓ `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`
- [x] Components section — ✓ "Who Is This For?" + "Acknowledgements" table
- [x] Usage examples — ✓ Before/After Python example + Quick Start
- [x] Quick Start — ✓
- [x] References user documentation — ✓ Links to online docs at `armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/`
- [x] License info — ✓
- [x] Contributor acknowledgements — ✓

README is high-quality — opens with problem framing, shows concrete before/after code, links to published docs, credits upstream dependencies. Exceeds BMB's baseline requirements.

**User Documentation (`docs/` folder):**

- **Location:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/docs/` (repo root, not `src/docs/`)
- **Documentation files:** 9 files

**Docs Contents:**

- `index.md` — Landing page with hero, quick install, doc navigation
- `getting-started.md` — Install, prerequisites, first skill
- `concepts.md` — Plain-English definitions
- `how-it-works.md` — Architecture, capability tiers, design decisions
- `workflows.md` — All workflows with commands
- `agents.md` — Ferris documentation
- `examples.md` — Real-world scenarios and troubleshooting
- `bmad-synergy.md` — BMAD integration cross-reference guide (recent addition, per git log)
- `404.md` — Not-found page for the Astro Starlight site

Docs are published to `armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/` via GitHub Pages with Starlight theme.

**Docs Quality:**

- [x] User-facing documentation — ✓ comprehensive
- [x] Getting-started guide — ✓
- [x] Concepts glossary — ✓ `concepts.md`
- [x] Agents documented by name — ✓ `agents.md`
- [x] Workflows documented — ✓ `workflows.md`
- [x] Examples provided — ✓ `examples.md`
- [x] Configuration documented — ✓ in getting-started (install prompts cover module.yaml variables)
- [x] Integration / ecosystem context — ✓ `bmad-synergy.md`

**Cross-references:**

- `workflows.md` says "12 workflows" in README and index — but there are **14** built workflows as of this validation (added `rename-skill` and `drop-skill` in the management category). Count mismatch. (Note: this is also present in `README.md:83` and `docs/index.md:44`.)

**Module-internal doc:**

- `src/workflows/README.md` (174 lines) — excellent internal doc, lists all 14 workflows, typical flows, knowledge/data distinction, directory structure — **correctly reports 14 workflows**
- `src/forger/README.md` (17 lines) — sidecar doc

**Issues Found:**

1. **⚠️ Workflow count mismatch in public docs** — `README.md` (line ~83) and `docs/index.md` (line ~44) state **"12 workflows"** but the module actually ships **14 workflows** (added `rename-skill` and `drop-skill` as management workflows). `src/workflows/README.md` correctly lists all 14. This is a visible inconsistency for anyone comparing the published docs against the agent menu (which has 14 workflow triggers + 2 action triggers). **Recommendation:** update both public-doc references to "14 workflows" — or better, make the number data-driven if the doc generator supports it.
2. **ℹ️ No `TODO.md`** — BMB template expects one; SKF doesn't have one. Acceptable for a mature module (roadmap lives in issues / claude-mem / CHANGELOG) but worth acknowledging.
3. **ℹ️ `docs/` at repo root, not in module dir** — Since SKF is a standalone npm module repo, this is appropriate (the Starlight site is repo-level, not module-level). However, from a strict BMB perspective, the spec looks for `{module}/docs/`. No action needed — the pattern fits SKF's distribution model.

**Recommendations:**

- Fix the "12 workflows" → "14 workflows" string in `README.md` line 83 and `docs/index.md` line 44 (and anywhere else it appears in `docs/workflows.md`).
- Consider a git pre-commit hook that validates the workflow count in public docs against the actual `src/workflows/` directory count.

**Exact out-of-date strings found:**

- `README.md:98` — "All **12** workflows with commands and connection diagrams"
- `docs/index.md:44` — "All **12** workflows with commands and connection diagrams"
- `docs/workflows.md:8` — **correctly says "14 workflows"** ✓
- `CHANGELOG.md:14` — "**12 workflows** — full lifecycle..." (historical release note; may be legitimately frozen at the release state, but worth reviewing)

lines: - `CHANGELOG.md:14` — "**12 workflows** — full lifecycle..." (historical release note; may be legitimately frozen at the release state, but worth reviewing)
+**Status:** PASS (with one minor CSV consistency note)
+- [x] `skills_output_folder` — prompt ✓, sensible default (`"skills"`), path template uses `{project-root}/{value}` ✓
+- [x] `forge_data_folder` — prompt ✓, sensible default (`"forge-data"`), path template uses `{project-root}/{value}` ✓
+- [x] `sidecar_path` — `prompt: false` (internal constant), default (`"_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar"`), path template uses `{project-root}/{value}` ✓

+- [x] `sidecar_path` is intentionally not prompted — protected by the agent's `critical_actions` GUARD that hard-halts with a user-actionable error if the install didn't resolve it (see `forger.agent.yaml:44`). This is defensive programming against a known installer edge case with `prompt: false` variables.
+- [x] All entries use `phase=anytime` with empty `sequence` column — ✓ (no sequenced/phased workflows, appropriate for lifecycle-driven module)
+- [x] 16 total entries — 14 workflow entries + 2 action entries (WS, KI)
+- [x] All 14 workflows from `src/workflows/` present in CSV — ✓ (SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, EX, RS, DS, VS, RA)

+- [x] Options column consistently tags mode (Create Mode, Surgeon Mode, Audit Mode, Delivery Mode, Management Mode, Architect Mode, Reference) — ✓ aligns with agent persona's four-mode model + Management & Reference
+- [x] Standalone module (no base to merge into) — ✓
+- [x] `code: skf` is unique (verified against common BMAD module codes: bmm, cis, bmgd, bmb — no conflict)
+- [x] `default_selected: false` appropriate for a specialized standalone module

+1. **ℹ️ `KI` (Knowledge Index) entry has non-empty `workflow-file`** — Line 17 points `workflow-file` at `_bmad/skf/knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv`, which is a CSV, not a workflow. In `forger.agent.yaml` the KI trigger is defined as an `action:` (inline) rather than a `workflow:`, so per the BMB spec ("Agent-only entries have EMPTY `workflow-file`") this cell should be empty. Functionally harmless (nothing reads it as a workflow) but technically out of spec. **Recommendation:** blank the `workflow-file` column for the KI row, or add a comment in the row that the file is the knowledge index CSV the action reads.

+The module is installable via `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`. All required structural and configuration elements are in place. The only open items are advisory (docs count mismatch, CSV KI row cosmetic fix, optional `src/README.md`).

- [x] `sidecar_path` — `prompt: false` (internal constant), default (`"_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar"`), path template uses `{project-root}/{value}` ✓

**Path variables:**

- [x] All 3 paths use `{project-root}/` prefix — ✓
- [x] Output paths (`skills_output_folder`, `forge_data_folder`) are user-configurable — ✓
- [x] `sidecar_path` is intentionally not prompted — protected by the agent's `critical_actions` GUARD that hard-halts with a user-actionable error if the install didn't resolve it (see `forger.agent.yaml:44`). This is defensive programming against a known installer edge case with `prompt: false` variables.

**Help Registry (`module-help.csv`):** ✓ present

- [x] Exists at `src/module-help.csv` — ✓
- [x] Header row valid — ✓ matches expected: `module,phase,name,code,sequence,workflow-file,command,required,agent,options,description,output-location,outputs,`
- [x] All entries use `phase=anytime` with empty `sequence` column — ✓ (no sequenced/phased workflows, appropriate for lifecycle-driven module)
- [x] 16 total entries — 14 workflow entries + 2 action entries (WS, KI)
- [x] All 14 workflows from `src/workflows/` present in CSV — ✓ (SF, AN, BS, CS, QS, SS, US, AS, TS, EX, RS, DS, VS, RA)
- [x] Agent names consistent (`ferris`) — ✓
- [x] Output locations reference module variables (`skills_output_folder`, `forge_data_folder`, `output_folder`) — ✓
- [x] Options column consistently tags mode (Create Mode, Surgeon Mode, Audit Mode, Delivery Mode, Management Mode, Architect Mode, Reference) — ✓ aligns with agent persona's four-mode model + Management & Reference

**Module Type Installation:**

- [x] Standalone module (no base to merge into) — ✓
- [x] `code: skf` is unique (verified against common BMAD module codes: bmm, cis, bmgd, bmb — no conflict)
- [x] `default_selected: false` appropriate for a specialized standalone module

**Issues Found:**

1. **ℹ️ `KI` (Knowledge Index) entry has non-empty `workflow-file`** — Line 17 points `workflow-file` at `_bmad/skf/knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv`, which is a CSV, not a workflow. In `forger.agent.yaml` the KI trigger is defined as an `action:` (inline) rather than a `workflow:`, so per the BMB spec ("Agent-only entries have EMPTY `workflow-file`") this cell should be empty. Functionally harmless (nothing reads it as a workflow) but technically out of spec. **Recommendation:** blank the `workflow-file` column for the KI row, or add a comment in the row that the file is the knowledge index CSV the action reads.

**Ready to Install:** ✅ **YES**

The module is installable via `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install`. All required structural and configuration elements are in place. The only open items are advisory (docs count mismatch, CSV KI row cosmetic fix, optional `src/README.md`).

+  - `forger.agent.yaml` — Ferris, Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian (icon ⚒️, sidecar-backed)

+  - setup-forge (SF), analyze-source (AN), brief-skill (BS), create-skill (CS), quick-skill (QS), create-stack-skill (SS), verify-stack (VS), refine-architecture (RA), update-skill (US), audit-skill (AS), test-skill (TS), export-skill (EX), rename-skill (RS), drop-skill (DS)
+1. **Update workflow count in public-facing docs** (user-visible inconsistency)
+   - Consider a pre-commit hook that asserts the count matches `ls src/workflows/*/workflow.md | wc -l`.

+2. **Add `src/README.md` for BMAD module-dir compliance** (if this module is ever packaged as a subdirectory inside another BMAD repo, the module-level README is what users of that context will see). Can be a minimal pointer to the repo-root README, or a mirror of the key sections.

+3. **Blank the `workflow-file` cell for the KI row in `module-help.csv:17`** — KI is an inline action, not a workflow. Per BMB spec, agent-only entries should have empty `workflow-file`. Currently points to the knowledge index CSV, which is benign but non-standard.

+4. **Document the `step-NNx-*.md` sub-step convention** — add a brief note in `src/workflows/README.md` explaining that letter suffixes (b, c, d) denote tier-gated sub-steps conditionally loaded by their parent step. Makes the convention discoverable for new contributors.

+5. **Watch step file sizes** — `export-skill/step-04-update-context.md` (399 lines), `rename-skill/step-02-execute.md` (391 lines), `drop-skill/step-01-select.md` (298 lines) are approaching the pragmatic JiT loading limit (~400 lines). Consider splitting if they grow further.

+6. **Non-standard `module.yaml` fields** — Either submit `description:` and `prompt: false` upstream to BMB as documented patterns, or acknowledge them as SKF extensions in an internal design doc, so future contributors understand they're intentional.

+7. **Review CHANGELOG.md:14 workflow count** — "12 workflows" in the release note may be historically frozen, but confirm it reflects the release it belongs to (and not an outdated statement about current state).
+The following built agents can be validated in detail using the agent validation workflow:

+Note: 10 of these already have recent individual validation reports on disk (`validation-report-{workflow}-2026-03-26.md`). The 4 workflows without recent individual reports are: **brief-skill**, **drop-skill**, **rename-skill**, and the 2026-03-26 batch may predate drop-skill/rename-skill. Prioritize running individual validations on those.
+Sub-processes can run in parallel for efficiency.
+1. **Fix P2 items** — update workflow count in README.md and docs/index.md (1-line change each) before the next release.
+3. **Re-run this validation** after fixes to confirm clean PASS.
+4. **Optionally run sub-process validation** on the forger agent and the 4 workflows lacking recent individual reports.

# Skill Forge (SKF) **Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow.** [![Quality & Validation](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml) [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) [![BMad Module](https://img.shields.io/badge/BMad-module-blue)](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) [![Docs](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-online-green)](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/) [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge?style=social)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/stargazers) *Skill Forge analyzes your code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse to build verified instruction files for AI agents. Every instruction links back to where it came from — nothing is made up.* **If SKF helps your agent stop hallucinating, give it a ⭐ — it helps others find this tool.**
--- ## The Problem You ask an AI agent to use a library. It invents function names that don't exist. It guesses parameter types. You paste documentation into the context — it still gets details wrong. You write instructions by hand — they go stale the moment the code changes. This isn't an edge case. It's the default experience. ## How Skill Forge Fixes This 1. **Analyzes your sources** — extracts real function signatures, types, and patterns from code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse 2. **Compiles verified instruction files** — every instruction links to the exact file and line it came from 3. **Version-aware** — skills are stored per-version, so updating to v2.0 doesn't break your v1.x skill. Compatible with [skill.sh](https://skill.sh) and [npx skills](https://www.npmjs.com/package/skills) 4. **Manageable lifecycle** — rename skills and drop deprecated versions without manual file surgery. Transactional safety for destructive operations. 5. **Follows an open standard** — skills comply with the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) spec and work across Claude, Cursor, Copilot, and other AI agents ## Before vs After **Without SKF** — your agent guesses: ```python import cognee # Agent hallucinates: sync call, wrong parameter name, missing await results = cognee.search("What does Cognee do?", mode="graph") ``` **With SKF** — your agent reads the verified skill: ```python import cognee # Agent follows the skill instruction: # `search(query_text: str, query_type: SearchType = GRAPH_COMPLETION) -> List[SearchResult]` # [AST:cognee/api/v1/search/search.py:L26] results = await cognee.search( query_text="What does Cognee do?", query_type=cognee.SearchType.GRAPH_COMPLETION ) ``` The skill told the agent the real function name, the real parameters, and that the call requires `await` — all traced to the exact source line. This is from a [real generated skill](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills). ## Install Requires [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22. ```bash npx bmad-module-skill-forge install ``` You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and IDE configuration. See the [docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/) for other install methods. ## Quick Start 1. **Set up your environment:** `@Ferris SF` — detects your tools and sets your capability tier 2. **Generate your first skill:** `@Ferris QS ` — creates a verified skill in under a minute 3. **Full quality path:** `@Ferris BS` → clear session → `@Ferris CS` — brief first, then compile for maximum accuracy > **Tip:** Start a fresh conversation before each workflow. SKF workflows load significant context — clearing between them prevents interference. See the [workflows docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/) for all 12 available workflows. ## Who Is This For? - **You use AI agents to write code** and they keep getting API calls wrong — hallucinating function names, guessing parameter types, inventing methods that don't exist - **You maintain a library** and want to ship official, verified instruction files so AI agents use your API correctly - **You manage a codebase with many dependencies** and want a consolidated "stack skill" that teaches your agent how all the pieces fit together - **You use a SaaS API or closed-source tool** with no public code — SKF can generate skills from documentation alone - **You need different skills for different use cases** from the same target — compile multiple skills with different scopes from one repo or doc set (e.g., a core API skill and a migration guide skill) ## Learn More - **[Getting Started](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/)** — Installation, prerequisites, and your first skill - **[Concepts](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/concepts/)** — Plain-English definitions of all key terms - **[How It Works](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/how-it-works/)** — Architecture, capability tiers, output format, and design decisions - **[Workflows](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/)** — All 12 workflows with commands and connection diagrams - **[Agents](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/agents/)** — Ferris: the AI agent that runs all SKF workflows - **[Examples](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/examples/)** — Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting ## Acknowledgements SKF builds on these excellent open-source tools: | Tool | Role in SKF | |--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| | [agentskills.io](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) | Skill specification and ecosystem standard | | [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | Source code access and repository intelligence (all tiers) | | [ast-grep](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep) | AST-based structural code extraction (Forge/Forge+/Deep tiers) | | [ast-grep MCP](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep-mcp) | MCP server for memory-efficient AST queries (recommended) | | [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) | Semantic code search and file discovery pre-ranking (Forge+ tier) | | [QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd) | Local hybrid search engine for knowledge indexing (Deep tier) | | [skill-check](https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check) | Skill validation, auto-fix, quality scoring, and security scanning | | [Snyk Agent Scan](https://github.com/snyk/agent-scan) | Security scanning for prompt injection and data exposure (optional) | | [tessl](https://tessl.io) | Content quality review, actionability scoring, and AI judge evaluation | | [BMad Method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) | Agent-workflow framework that SKF extends as a module | ## Contributing See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines. ## License MIT License — see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details. --- **Skill Forge (SKF)** — A standalone [BMad](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module for agent skill compilation. [![Contributors](https://contrib.rocks/image?repo=armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/graphs/contributors) See [CONTRIBUTORS.md](CONTRIBUTORS.md) for contributor information.

title: Skill Forge (SKF)
description: Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow
template: splash
hero:
  title: Skill Forge (SKF)
  tagline: Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow.
  actions:
    - text: Getting Started
      link: ./getting-started/
      icon: right-arrow
      variant: primary
    - text: View on GitHub
      link: https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge
      icon: external
---

## What does Skill Forge do?

AI agents hallucinate API calls. They invent function names, guess parameter types, and produce code that doesn't compile. Skill Forge fixes this by analyzing code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse — extracting real signatures and patterns and compiling them into verified instruction files that any AI agent can follow. Every instruction traces back to where it came from. Generated skills can also include executable scripts and static assets extracted from the source repository, each with full provenance tracking.

## Quick Install

Requires [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22.

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Then set up your environment and generate your first skill:

```
@Ferris SF              # Set up your forge
@Ferris QS <package>    # Generate a skill in under a minute
```

See the [Getting Started](./getting-started/) guide for full installation and usage instructions.

## Documentation

- **[Getting Started](./getting-started/)** — Installation, prerequisites, and your first skill
- **[Concepts](./concepts/)** — Plain-English definitions of key terms (provenance, tiers, drift, and more)
- **[How It Works](./how-it-works/)** — Architecture, capability model, output format, and design decisions
- **[Workflows](./workflows/)** — All 12 workflows with commands and connection diagrams
- **[Agents](./agents/)** — Ferris: the AI agent that runs all SKF workflows
- **[Examples](./examples/)** — Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting

## Support

- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/issues)

# Skill Forge (SKF) **Turn code and docs into instructions AI agents can actually follow.** [![Quality & Validation](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/actions/workflows/quality.yaml) [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge) [![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT) [![BMad Module](https://img.shields.io/badge/BMad-module-blue)](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) [![Docs](https://img.shields.io/badge/docs-online-green)](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/) [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge?style=social)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/stargazers) *Skill Forge analyzes your code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse to build verified instruction files for AI agents. Every instruction links back to where it came from — nothing is made up.* **If SKF helps your agent stop hallucinating, give it a ⭐ — it helps others find this tool.**
--- ## The Problem You ask an AI agent to use a library. It invents function names that don't exist. It guesses parameter types. You paste documentation into the context — it still gets details wrong. You write instructions by hand — they go stale the moment the code changes. This isn't an edge case. It's the default experience. ## How Skill Forge Fixes This 1. **Analyzes your sources** — extracts real function signatures, types, and patterns from code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse 2. **Compiles verified instruction files** — every instruction links to the exact file and line it came from 3. **Version-aware** — skills are stored per-version, so updating to v2.0 doesn't break your v1.x skill. Compatible with [skill.sh](https://skill.sh) and [npx skills](https://www.npmjs.com/package/skills) 4. **Manageable lifecycle** — rename skills and drop deprecated versions without manual file surgery. Transactional safety for destructive operations. 5. **Follows an open standard** — skills comply with the [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io) spec and work across Claude, Cursor, Copilot, and other AI agents ## Before vs After **Without SKF** — your agent guesses: ```python import cognee # Agent hallucinates: sync call, wrong parameter name, missing await results = cognee.search("What does Cognee do?", mode="graph") ``` **With SKF** — your agent reads the verified skill: ```python import cognee # Agent follows the skill instruction: # `search(query_text: str, query_type: SearchType = GRAPH_COMPLETION) -> List[SearchResult]` # [AST:cognee/api/v1/search/search.py:L26] results = await cognee.search( query_text="What does Cognee do?", query_type=cognee.SearchType.GRAPH_COMPLETION ) ``` The skill told the agent the real function name, the real parameters, and that the call requires `await` — all traced to the exact source line. This is from a [real generated skill](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/oh-my-skills). ## Install Requires [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/) >= 22. ```bash npx bmad-module-skill-forge install ``` You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and IDE configuration. See the [docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/) for other install methods. ## Quick Start 1. **Set up your environment:** `@Ferris SF` — detects your tools and sets your capability tier 2. **Generate your first skill:** `@Ferris QS ` — creates a verified skill in under a minute 3. **Full quality path:** `@Ferris BS` → clear session → `@Ferris CS` — brief first, then compile for maximum accuracy > **Tip:** Start a fresh conversation before each workflow. SKF workflows load significant context — clearing between them prevents interference. See the [workflows docs](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/) for all 12 available workflows. ## Who Is This For? - **You use AI agents to write code** and they keep getting API calls wrong — hallucinating function names, guessing parameter types, inventing methods that don't exist - **You maintain a library** and want to ship official, verified instruction files so AI agents use your API correctly - **You manage a codebase with many dependencies** and want a consolidated "stack skill" that teaches your agent how all the pieces fit together - **You use a SaaS API or closed-source tool** with no public code — SKF can generate skills from documentation alone - **You need different skills for different use cases** from the same target — compile multiple skills with different scopes from one repo or doc set (e.g., a core API skill and a migration guide skill) ## Learn More - **[Getting Started](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/getting-started/)** — Installation, prerequisites, and your first skill - **[Concepts](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/concepts/)** — Plain-English definitions of all key terms - **[How It Works](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/how-it-works/)** — Architecture, capability tiers, output format, and design decisions - **[Workflows](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/workflows/)** — All 14 workflows with commands and connection diagrams - **[Agents](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/agents/)** — Ferris: the AI agent that runs all SKF workflows - **[Examples](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/examples/)** — Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting ## Acknowledgements SKF builds on these excellent open-source tools: | Tool | Role in SKF | |--------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------| | [agentskills.io](https://github.com/agentskills/agentskills) | Skill specification and ecosystem standard | | [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) | Source code access and repository intelligence (all tiers) | | [ast-grep](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep) | AST-based structural code extraction (Forge/Forge+/Deep tiers) | | [ast-grep MCP](https://github.com/ast-grep/ast-grep-mcp) | MCP server for memory-efficient AST queries (recommended) | | [cocoindex-code](https://github.com/cocoindex-io/cocoindex-code) | Semantic code search and file discovery pre-ranking (Forge+ tier) | | [QMD](https://github.com/tobi/qmd) | Local hybrid search engine for knowledge indexing (Deep tier) | | [skill-check](https://github.com/thedaviddias/skill-check) | Skill validation, auto-fix, quality scoring, and security scanning | | [Snyk Agent Scan](https://github.com/snyk/agent-scan) | Security scanning for prompt injection and data exposure (optional) | | [tessl](https://tessl.io) | Content quality review, actionability scoring, and AI judge evaluation | | [BMad Method](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) | Agent-workflow framework that SKF extends as a module | ## Contributing See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for guidelines. ## License MIT License — see [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details. --- **Skill Forge (SKF)** — A standalone [BMad](https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD) module for agent skill compilation. [![Contributors](https://contrib.rocks/image?repo=armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge)](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/graphs/contributors) See [CONTRIBUTORS.md](CONTRIBUTORS.md) for contributor information.

Workflow count fixes landed. Three locations updated:
- `README.md:83` — "all 12 available workflows" → "all 14 available workflows"
- `README.md:98` — "All 12 workflows" → "All 14 workflows"
- `docs/index.md:44` — "All 12 workflows" → "All 14 workflows"

`CHANGELOG.md:14` left alone (historical release note for an earlier version).

Now for the optional `src/README.md` — do you want me to:

**(a)** Create a minimal `src/README.md` that lives inside the module dir (acts as a pointer to the repo-root README with the module-level specifics: what's in `src/`, how agents and workflows are organized), or
**(b)** Skip it — since `docs/` and repo-root `README.md` already cover user-facing documentation and you distribute as an npm module where `src/` is an implementation detail, and the BMB standard targeting `{module}/README.md` doesn't really fit a standalone-repo distribution model.

My recommendation is **(b) skip** — the BMB `README.md` requirement assumes the `src/modules/{code}/` monorepo layout; for a standalone npm-distributed module the repo-root README is the canonical location. But your call.

Which do you want?

# forger-sidecar

This folder stores persistent memory for the **Ferris** agent (Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian).

## Purpose

Cross-session state for the SKF module's skill compilation lifecycle.

## Files

- `preferences.yaml` — User preferences: language defaults, output format settings
- `forge-tier.yaml` — Tool availability (ast-grep, gh, QMD, ccc), derived capability tier, CCC index state, and QMD collection registry

## Runtime Access

After BMAD installation, this folder will be accessible at:
`{project-root}/_bmad/_memory/forger-sidecar/`

input.content:
# SKF Module Source

This directory contains the **Skill Forge (SKF)** BMAD module — the files that get installed into a BMAD project under `_bmad/skf/` when a user runs the installer.

For user-facing documentation (what SKF does, how to install it, how to use it), see the [repository README](../README.md) and the published docs at [armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/).

## Layout

```
src/
├── module.yaml           # Module config: code, name, install prompts
├── module-help.csv       # Help registry — one row per agent command
├── agents/
│   └── forger.agent.yaml # Ferris — the single agent persona
├── workflows/            # 14 workflows (see workflows/README.md)
│   ├── setup-forge/
│   ├── analyze-source/
│   ├── brief-skill/
│   ├── create-skill/
│   ├── quick-skill/
│   ├── create-stack-skill/
│   ├── verify-stack/
│   ├── refine-architecture/
│   ├── update-skill/
│   ├── audit-skill/
│   ├── test-skill/
│   ├── export-skill/
│   ├── rename-skill/
│   └── drop-skill/
├── knowledge/            # Cross-cutting knowledge fragments (JiT loaded)
│   └── skf-knowledge-index.csv
└── forger/               # Sidecar seed files (preferences, forge tier)
```

## Components

- **Agent:** [Ferris](agents/forger.agent.yaml) — single-persona module operating in four modes (Architect / Surgeon / Audit / Delivery)
- **Workflows:** see [workflows/README.md](workflows/README.md) for the full index, typical flows, and the knowledge-vs-data distinction
- **Knowledge fragments:** cross-cutting principles Ferris consults via `knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv`

## Editing this module

- Agent edits — `agents/forger.agent.yaml`; validate with `bmad:bmb:agents:agent-validate`
- Workflow edits — each workflow in `workflows/{name}/`; validate with `bmad:bmb:workflows:workflow-validate`
- Module-level edits — `module.yaml` and `module-help.csv`; validate with `bmad:bmb:modules:validate-module`

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md) for the contribution workflow.

toolUseResult.content:
# SKF Module Source

This directory contains the **Skill Forge (SKF)** BMAD module — the files that get installed into a BMAD project under `_bmad/skf/` when a user runs the installer.

For user-facing documentation (what SKF does, how to install it, how to use it), see the [repository README](../README.md) and the published docs at [armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge](https://armelhbobdad.github.io/bmad-module-skill-forge/).

## Layout

```
src/
├── module.yaml           # Module config: code, name, install prompts
├── module-help.csv       # Help registry — one row per agent command
├── agents/
│   └── forger.agent.yaml # Ferris — the single agent persona
├── workflows/            # 14 workflows (see workflows/README.md)
│   ├── setup-forge/
│   ├── analyze-source/
│   ├── brief-skill/
│   ├── create-skill/
│   ├── quick-skill/
│   ├── create-stack-skill/
│   ├── verify-stack/
│   ├── refine-architecture/
│   ├── update-skill/
│   ├── audit-skill/
│   ├── test-skill/
│   ├── export-skill/
│   ├── rename-skill/
│   └── drop-skill/
├── knowledge/            # Cross-cutting knowledge fragments (JiT loaded)
│   └── skf-knowledge-index.csv
└── forger/               # Sidecar seed files (preferences, forge tier)
```

## Components

- **Agent:** [Ferris](agents/forger.agent.yaml) — single-persona module operating in four modes (Architect / Surgeon / Audit / Delivery)
- **Workflows:** see [workflows/README.md](workflows/README.md) for the full index, typical flows, and the knowledge-vs-data distinction
- **Knowledge fragments:** cross-cutting principles Ferris consults via `knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv`

## Editing this module

- Agent edits — `agents/forger.agent.yaml`; validate with `bmad:bmb:agents:agent-validate`
- Workflow edits — each workflow in `workflows/{name}/`; validate with `bmad:bmb:workflows:workflow-validate`
- Module-level edits — `module.yaml` and `module-help.csv`; validate with `bmad:bmb:modules:validate-module`

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](../CONTRIBUTING.md) for the contribution workflow.

docs: correct workflow count and add module source orientation

shipped 14 since rename-skill (RS) and drop-skill (DS) were added.
